title: Octets, Byte Streams, Fragmentation, Buffering, and Partial I/O · Go Build Room
summary: A protocol pairing room predicts byte, parser, state, ownership, and cleanup transitions before assembling the first fragment-safe byte-stream reader and writer contract for octets, byte streams, fragmentation, buffering, and partial i/o. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of fragment-safe byte-stream reader and writer contract while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence…
objectives: Keep protocol data as octets until grammar permits text interpretation and distinguish ASCII tokens, opaque content, and invalid control bytes.
Interpret Read results using both byte count and error, including data with io.EOF, zero-byte reads, and temporary fragmentation.
Complete bounded output across short writes while retaining progress, error, and ownership evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Octet Text Boundary
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop protocol team handles case 2020 in a municipal gateway receiving one-byte TCP fragments. Build deterministic Go evidence to preserve partial bytes while distinguishing fragmentation from incomplete input; preserve this operating constraint: the maintained Go protocol stack remains the production transfer target; then truncate one content octet and classify incomplete framing without connection reuse. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Octet Text Boundary.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for keep protocol data as octets until grammar permits text interpretation and distinguish ascii tokens, opaque content, and invalid control bytes. For Commit to the mission model · Workshop Octet Text Boundary, keep Octet Text Boundary connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

lines.text: A parser that passes one whole-buffer test is independent of network fragmentation.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Prove the same bounded message result for every legal segmentation of an identical byte sequence.

options.text: For test an acceptance boundary, run one convenient Fragmentation Invariance happy path, omit ownership, error, changed input, goroutine stop, and full-toolchain checks, then accept plausible output. This does not satisfy Fragmentation Invariance for fragment-safe byte-stream reader and writer contract.
Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Fragmentation Invariance condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.

Keep the plausible Fragmentation Invariance output and hide the violated fragment-safe byte-stream reader and writer contract assumption instead of explaining the failure. That claim fails the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary for Fragmentation Invariance.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model · Workshop Fragmentation Invariance
steps.instruction:
Write the workshop evidence note for Fragmentation Invariance: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A workshop protocol team handles case 2029 in a protocol upgrade gateway transferring buffered tunnel bytes. Build deterministic Go evidence to preserve partial bytes while distinguishing fragmentation from incomplete input; preserve this operating constraint: one parser state transition may consume bytes only after its full precondition holds; then force a short write and retain the exact transmitted prefix plus remaining count. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Prove the same bounded message result for every legal segmentation of an identical byte sequence.

Evidence target: Defend the transferred model · Workshop Fragmentation Invariance.
steps.why: Retrieving Fragmentation Invariance through the concrete fragment-safe byte-stream reader and writer contract case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For Defend the transferred model · Workshop Fragmentation Invariance, keep Fragmentation Invariance connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Prove the same bounded message result for every legal segmentation of an identical byte sequence.

hints: For Defend the transferred model · Workshop Fragmentation Invariance, name the types, ownership, and stop path, Fragmentation Invariance precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Defend the transferred model · Workshop Fragmentation Invariance, trace this concrete input and predict values, errors, blocking, and output before reviewing it: A workshop protocol team handles case 2029 in a protocol upgrade gateway transferring buffered tunnel bytes. Build deterministic Go evidence to preserve partial bytes while distinguishing fragmentation from incomplete input; preserve this operating constraint: one parser state transition may consume bytes only after its full precondition holds; then force a short write and retain the exact transmitted prefix plus remaining count. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Prove the same bounded message result for every legal segmentation of an identical byte sequence.

For Defend the transferred model · Workshop Fragmentation Invariance, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: A parser that passes one whole-buffer test is independent of network fragmentation.
